High winds combined with the heavy snow during the afternoon of the 2nd leading to blizzard conditions from Otsego and Delaware Counties to Sullivan County, New York. Winds were frequently gusting to 50 mph. The rest of central NY saw near blizzard conditions during this time. The snow tapered down in the evening as the winds continued to cause near whiteout conditions, knocking down numerous trees and power lines from Otsego County to Sullivan County.  An estimated 40,000 customers were without power mainly in the Sullivan County area where a State of Emergency was declared. Power was out in some areas for more than one week. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet in most areas with the highest amounts from Oneida County south to the western Catskills in central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulated between 8 and 12 inches. There was also considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system brought widespread moderate to heavy snowfall mainly north and west of the Capital Region with a mixture of rain and snow with light accumulations south and east. Snow overspread the region during the morning, fell heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening and started to taper off from west to east in the late evening hours. Snowfall totals ranged from 6.0 to 15.0 inches across the Adirondacks, Mohawk Valley and the Lake George-Saratoga Region. For locations south and east of the Capital District, snowfall totals ranged from a trace to 5.0 inches.

A wave of low pressure developed across the southeastern states and tracked towards the northeast on February 7, 2018. The low brought mainly light snow and some light freezing rain. The low pressure tracked a long a warm front which lifted across portions of the region helping to change any snow to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters reported 2 to 3 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Tennessee Valley early on the 7th to the New Jersey coast by the late afternoon of the 7th to off the New England coast by the late evening. This low brought a heavy snow to a large part of central New York on the 7th. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 12 inches over many locations in central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening low pressure system brought some light snow to eastern New York during the early to mid-morning hours of March 7th, 2018. Meanwhile, a Nor'easter strengthened rapidly along the Atlantic Coast, which resulted in increasing snow intensity during the afternoon and evening hours. Heavy snow bands rotated northwestward and stalled across portions of the Mid-Hudson Valley and Taconics, resulting in one to two feet of accumulation by the morning of March 8th.  West of the heaviest bands, 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulated across much of the Capital District, eastern Catskills, and Lake George-Saratoga Region. The heavy snowfall resulted in the closure of Interstates 84 and 88 and the New York State Thruway to high-profile vehicles. This event was the second major winter storm in less than a week.

A complex area of low pressure tracked from the Ohio Valley and southeast United States during the afternoon of the 1st and intensified into a major winter storm over Long Island by the morning of the 2nd. This intense winter storm remained nearly stationary until the late evening of the 2nd. The storm brought rain during the evening of the 1st in central New York, which changed to snow first in the Finger Lakes and Steuben County before midnight of the 2nd. The rain then turned to all snow across the rest of central New York during the early morning hours of the 2nd. The snow fell heavy at times through the day on the 2nd. High winds combined with the heavy snow during the afternoon of the 2nd leading to blizzard conditions from Otsego and Delaware Counties to Sullivan County, New York. Winds were frequently gusting to 50 mph. The rest of central NY saw near blizzard conditions during this time. The snow tapered down in the evening as the winds continued to cause near whiteout conditions, knocking down numerous trees and power lines from Otsego County to Sullivan County.  An estimated 40,000 customers were without power mainly in the Sullivan County area where a State of Emergency was declared. Power was out in some areas for more than one week. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet in most areas with the highest amounts from Oneida County south to the western Catskills in central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall combined with winds frequently gusting over 35 mph to create blizzard conditions. The winds were strongest during the afternoon and evening of the 2nd. There were trees knocked down along with power outages and considerable blowing and drifting snow. Whiteout conditions were common. Total snowfall ranged from 7 to 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure developed across the southeastern states and tracked towards the northeast on February 7, 2018. The low brought mainly light snow and some light freezing rain. The low pressure tracked a long a warm front which lifted across portions of the region helping to change any snow to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Central Park ASOS reported 0.09 inches of freezing rain.

A winter storm brought mixed wintry precipitation to the region. Precipitation began as snow during the morning hours but changed to a mix of sleet, freezing rain and snow during the afternoon hours, mixing as far north as the town of Schenectady. Precipitation transitioned back to snow during the evening hours before exiting the region northwest to southeast. Snowfall and sleet totals ranged from 1 inch to 12 inches, with the higher totals generally north and west of the Greater Capital District.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Larchmont Harbor reported a wind gust up to 55 mph at 551 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Ohio Valley during the evening of the 6th to off the New Jersey coast while intensifying by the morning of the 7th. The area of low pressure tracked well offshore by the evening of the 7th. This storm brought heavy snow to portions of northeast Pennsylvania and Sullivan County New York from the evening of the 6th to the early afternoon of the 7th. Snowfall ranged from 8 to 15 inches. The snow was heavy and wet prolonging the power outages that continued from the snowstorm of March 2nd. Some customers were without power for well over a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 7 and 12 inches.

A Noreaster tracked up the New England coast from March 7-9, 2018, bringing a long period of moderate snow across the eastern portions of the area. Upslope flow behind the system that enhanced amounts in the Chautauqua Ridge and Boston Hills toward the end of the period. Localized warning accumulations were realized in portion of the Lake Erie snow belts, including in Cattaraugus County -- Perrysburg 12, in Erie County -- Springville 16, and in Chautauqua County -- Clymer 14.7.

This intense winter storm remained nearly stationary until the late evening of the 2nd. The storm brought rain during the evening of the 1st in central New York, which changed to snow first in the Finger Lakes and Steuben County before midnight of the 2nd. The rain then turned to all snow across the rest of central New York during the early morning hours of the 2nd. The snow fell heavy at times through the day on the 2nd. High winds combined with the heavy snow during the afternoon of the 2nd leading to blizzard conditions from Otsego and Delaware Counties to Sullivan County, New York. Winds were frequently gusting to 50 mph. The rest of central NY saw near blizzard conditions during this time. The snow tapered down in the evening as the winds continued to cause near whiteout conditions, knocking down numerous trees and power lines from Otsego County to Sullivan County.  An estimated 40,000 customers were without power mainly in the Sullivan County area where a State of Emergency was declared. Power was out in some areas for more than one week. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet in most areas with the highest amounts from Oneida County south to the western Catskills in central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulated between 8 and 12 inches. There was also considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought mixed wintry precipitation to the region. Precipitation began as snow during the morning hours but changed to a mix of sleet, freezing rain and snow during the afternoon hours, mixing as far north as the town of Schenectady. Precipitation transitioned back to snow during the evening hours before exiting the region northwest to southeast. Snowfall and sleet totals ranged from 1 inch to 12 inches, with the higher totals generally north and west of the Greater Capital District.

A complex storm system brought widespread moderate to heavy snowfall mainly north and west of the Capital Region with a mixture of rain and snow with light accumulations south and east. Snow overspread the region during the morning, fell heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening and started to taper off from west to east in the late evening hours. Snowfall totals ranged from 6.0 to 15.0 inches across the Adirondacks, Mohawk Valley and the Lake George-Saratoga Region. For locations south and east of the Capital District, snowfall totals ranged from a trace to 5.0 inches.

A low pressure system rapidly strengthened as it moved from eastern New York to New Brunswick on November 3rd, resulting in strong winds across the region. Several downed trees and limbs, some resulting in road closures, were reported in the Capital District, while around 2,000 people lost power in the Mid-Hudson Valley. Gusts up to 50 mph were recorded.

Low pressure moved from the Tennessee valley across southern New England on February 7th. It was a quick but potent system with duration of 6 to 8 hours and snowfall rates of an inch or more for several hours. Snowfall accumulations of 5 to 10 inches were observed across much of northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across St. Lawrence county with a few reports exceeding 8 inches, including 8 inches at Cranberry Lake.

An area of low pressure rapidly intensified off the coast of Long Island on March 2nd, 2018. A large swath of tropical moisture was fed into the storm and wrapped back into the advancing cold air, resulting in heavy, wet snowfall across eastern New York. The snow began in the wee hours of March 2nd and quickly became heavy, with moderate to heavy snowfall continuing for much of the day before tapering off in the evening hours. The eastern Catskills and Schoharie Valley were the hardest-hit in terms of snowfall amounts, with 20 to 40 inches recorded. The heavy snowfall resulted in the closure of Interstate 88 to tractor trailers, buses, and high-profile vehicles. The snowfall varied drastically with elevation, as areas along the Hudson River near Saugerties remained mainly rain and saw no snowfall accumulation, while around 10 miles east and 200 feet higher, snowfall of 12 to 18 inches occurred.||Winds increased during the afternoon and evening, gusting to 35 to 50 mph across many areas. The combination of the heavy, wet snowfall and the winds resulted in downed trees and wires and power outages. Nearly 100,000 people were without power in eastern New York, with most outages in the Mid-Hudson Valley and eastern Catskill regions. In Dutchess County, 10,000 people were still without power three days after the storm, where a state of emergency was declared. The National Guard was deployed to assist with storm recovery.

The snowfall varied drastically with elevation, as areas along the Hudson River near Saugerties remained mainly rain and saw no snowfall accumulation, while around 10 miles east and 200 feet higher, snowfall of 12 to 18 inches occurred.||Winds increased during the afternoon and evening, gusting to 35 to 50 mph across many areas. The combination of the heavy, wet snowfall and the winds resulted in downed trees and wires and power outages. Nearly 100,000 people were without power in eastern New York, with most outages in the Mid-Hudson Valley and eastern Catskill regions. In Dutchess County, 10,000 people were still without power three days after the storm, where a state of emergency was declared. The National Guard was deployed to assist with storm recovery.

Another Nor'easter swept across eastern New England on March 13th and remained nearly stationary across eastern Quebec/Nova Scotia on the 14th-15th. This allowed a long duration snowfall event across New York with snow beginning during the morning of March 13th but the heaviest occurring during the night of March 13th through March 14th. Snow squalls impacted portions of the Mohawk Valley and Capital District during the early morning hours of November 14th. Though accumulation was minimal, the snow froze on area roadways prior to the morning rush hour, resulting in numerous accidents and lengthy traffic delays. The Interstate 90 bridge over the Hudson River was closed for several hours due to a crash. Some schools were delayed.

A coastal low developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast by the morning of March 7th and moved into the Gulf of Maine on March 8th, then closed and filled with the upper level low across New England. snow began by midday on March 7th and lingered with mountain snow showers on the 9th. Snowfall intensities were light to moderate at times with snowfall accumulations 3 to 8 inches across northern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event deposited 3 to 8 inches across Clinton county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system rapidly strengthened as it moved from eastern New York to New Brunswick on November 3rd, resulting in strong winds across the region. Several downed trees and limbs, some resulting in road closures, were reported in the Capital District, while around 2,000 people lost power in the Mid-Hudson Valley. Gusts up to 50 mph were recorded.

Gusts up to 50 mph were recorded.

A powerful low pressure system and a trailing cold front passed through the region on Wednesday, April 4th. Strong, damaging winds occurred both with the frontal passage but also behind it as northwest flow ensued. Wind gusts up to 60 mph occurred across the region which resulted in numerous trees and power lines down. Several thousand people were left without power with almost 10,000 people without power in Warren county alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 60 mph caused damage to a vinyl porch ceiling in the town of Hessville, New York. Additionally, there were measured wind gusts of 56 and 47 mph in the towns of Amsterdam and Tribes Hill, respectively.

Wind gusts up to 60 mph occurred across the region which resulted in numerous trees and power lines down. EVENT_NARRATIVE: One report of trees down onto wires which were on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a northerly flow funneling cold air into the region from Canada, light precipitation occurred along a stationary boundary.  This resulted in a wintry mix, especially for northern and high terrain areas, with a light accretion of ice.  Some slippery travel occurred as a result of this precipitation.   Behind this frontal boundary, the combination of a strong upper level low and surface low tracking south of the region allowed for an additional period of light wintry precipitation and rainfall to the region.  In addition, strong easterly winds associated with the storm led to strong winds across downslope favored areas of the Taconics. These high winds resulted in numerous trees downed and power outages, with wind gusts up to 74 mph being recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of trees and wires down due to high winds.

Heavy rainfall developed across the area on the morning of April 16th ahead of a slow moving warm front. This rain developed in an environment with precipitable water values greater than 1.25 inches, well above normal for mid April. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 1.5 to 3.5 inches across much of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley, with the majority of the rain falling in a 3-4 hour period. This resulted in flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mahwah River at Suffern rose above its flood stage of 4.0 feet at 11:00am EDT, crested at a height of 5.51 feet at 2:45pm EDT, and fell below flood stage at 7:15am EDT on April 17th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bronx River at the New York Botanical Garden rose above its flood stage of 3.0 feet at 9:45am EDT, crested at a height of 4.24 feet at 4:00pm EDT, and fell below flood stage at 10:00pm EDT. The crest of 4.24 feet is above major flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed in the Little Salmon River in Fort Covington in January and remained in place through February. In late February the combination of record warmth, rainfall, and rapid snow melt caused significant rises on the little Salmon River which was already jammed from January. This caused flooding of the town of Fort Convington and evacuations were performed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed in the Little Salmon River in Fort Covington in January and remained in place through February. In late February the combination of record warmth, rainfall, and rapid snow melt caused significant rises on the little Salmon River which was already jammed from January. At 1216pm notification was passed along that water evacuations were being performed which necessitated the upgrade from an areal flood warning to a flash flood warning in coordination with local emergency management.

Waves of low pressure moved along a stalled frontal boundary across the Middle Atlantic. Moderate to heavy snow fell during the morning commute across southeast New York. Snowfall rates reached 1 inch per hour at times. Daily record snowfall amounts were set at Central Park (5.0 inches), LaGuardia (5.5 inches), Kennedy (3.7 inches), and Islip (4.6 inches) for April 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the public reported 6 to 8 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted portions of the Mohawk Valley and Capital District during the early morning hours of November 14th. Though accumulation was minimal, the snow froze on area roadways prior to the morning rush hour, resulting in numerous accidents and lengthy traffic delays. The Interstate 90 bridge over the Hudson River was closed for several hours due to a crash. Some schools were delayed.

A strengthening coastal low pressure system moved along the Atlantic shoreline November 15th and 16th, bringing accumulating snow and mixed precipitation to eastern New York. The Mid-Hudson Valley and eastern Catskills were hit hardest with 8 to 14 inches of snow, most of which occurred during the evening hours. The snow spread in during the evening rush hour with one to three inches per hour accumulation, snarling traffic and resulting in numerous accidents. The southern Adirondacks also saw 6 to 12 inches of snowfall, while totals for the remainder of eastern New York generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches. The snow changed to sleet and freezing rain overnight in many areas with minimal ice accretion. It changed back to snow during the morning hours of the 16th before ending. The snow allowed many area ski hills to open earlier than normal for the season.

The Mid-Hudson Valley and eastern Catskills were hit hardest with 8 to 14 inches of snow, most of which occurred during the evening hours. The snow spread in during the evening rush hour with one to three inches per hour accumulation, snarling traffic and resulting in numerous accidents. The southern Adirondacks also saw 6 to 12 inches of snowfall, while totals for the remainder of eastern New York generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches. The snow changed to sleet and freezing rain overnight in many areas with minimal ice accretion. It changed back to snow during the morning hours of the 16th before ending. The snow allowed many area ski hills to open earlier than normal for the season.

An intense storm system tracked across the eastern Great Lakes region during the early morning hours of the 4th and then down the Saint Lawrence River Valley during the day and evening of the 4th. Strong winds behind this system spread across a large part of northern and western New York State including the Finger Lakes region of upstate New York from the late morning into the early evening. Numerous locations had wind gusts approaching 60 mph with trees and wires down in many locations. A church suffered some roof damage in the Greater Syracuse area. The wind gusts peaked during the late afternoon hours of the 4th before subsiding after sunset.

A slow moving frontal system brought heavy rain and easterly gale winds to the region during the times of high tide Monday morning into early Monday afternoon. Although waters levels only reached minor flood thresholds along lower NY Harbor and Long Island, heavy rains Monday morning across overwhelmed drainages caused widespread flash flooding across the region. Additionally, 5 to 8 ft breaking waves and elevated water levels resulted in minor to moderate erosion along the beachfront.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Orient Point State Park had moderate erosion along the Gardiners Bay shoreline due to high wave action during times of high tide Sunday Night and Monday Morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system and a trailing cold front passed through the region on Wednesday, April 4th. Strong, damaging winds occurred both with the frontal passage but also behind it as northwest flow ensued. Wind gusts up to 60 mph occurred across the region which resulted in numerous trees and power lines down. Several thousand people were left without power with almost 10,000 people without power in Warren county alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate reports of trees down in the town of Lagrange, New York. A wind gust of 51 mph was also reported near Pleasant Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a northerly flow funneling cold air into the region from Canada, light precipitation occurred along a stationary boundary.  This resulted in a wintry mix, especially for northern and high terrain areas, with a light accretion of ice.  Some slippery travel occurred as a result of this precipitation.   Behind this frontal boundary, the combination of a strong upper level low and surface low tracking south of the region allowed for an additional period of light wintry precipitation and rainfall to the region.  In addition, strong easterly winds associated with the storm led to strong winds across downslope favored areas of the Taconics. These high winds resulted in numerous trees downed and power outages, with wind gusts up to 74 mph being recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall developed across the area on the morning of April 16th ahead of a slow moving warm front. This rain developed in an environment with precipitable water values greater than 1.25 inches, well above normal for mid April. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 1.5 to 3.5 inches across much of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley, with the majority of the rain falling in a 3-4 hour period. This resulted in flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Henry Hudson Parkway was closed at West 237th Street in Riverdale due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a northerly flow funneling cold air into the region from Canada, light precipitation occurred along a stationary boundary.  This resulted in a wintry mix, especially for northern and high terrain areas, with a light accretion of ice.  Some slippery travel occurred as a result of this precipitation.   Behind this frontal boundary, the combination of a strong upper level low and surface low tracking south of the region allowed for an additional period of light wintry precipitation and rainfall to the region.  In addition, strong easterly winds associated with the storm led to strong winds across downslope favored areas of the Taconics. These high winds resulted in numerous trees downed and power outages, with wind gusts up to 74 mph being recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of small trees down across the southern portion of Herkimer county. New York State Mesonet sites recorded wind gusts of 40 and 43 mph in Herkimer and Cold Brook, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall developed across the area on the morning of April 16th ahead of a slow moving warm front. This rain developed in an environment with precipitable water values greater than 1.25 inches, well above normal for mid April. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 1.5 to 3.5 inches across much of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley, with the majority of the rain falling in a 3-4 hour period. This resulted in flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southbound lanes of the Henry Hudson Parkway were closed at West 96th Street in Bloomingdale due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a stationary front located just north of Long Island during the afternoon of September 12th. The 8am sounding from Upton, NY recorded a precipitable water value of 2.16 inches, a record for the date based on the Storm Prediction Center's sounding climatology. This resulted in very heavy downpours and flash flooding over parts of northern Suffolk County. A CoCoRaHS observer in Port Jefferson Station recorded 2.98 inches of rain over a period of about three and a half hours in this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in two homes on Martingale Court in Stony Brook resulted in electrical issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure and its associated warm front approached the region from the south, precipitation overspread the New York City metro area during the early morning hours of September 28th. With precipitable water values around 2 inches and much of the rain falling in just a few hours over an urban area, this resulted in isolated flash flooding in New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Linden Place was closed due to flooding north of I-678 (Whitestone Expressway) in College Point, Queens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region with temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the 60s and 70s.  This resulted in heat index values reaching 100 to 110 degrees during the afternoon hours in valley areas. ||This hot and humid air mass also provided an unstable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms to form ahead of a cold front passage during the afternoon and evening hours of June 18th. The storms caused numerous trees and power lines to be knocked down, resulting in over 10,000 customers without power. A vacant historic building in Poughkeepsie, New York partially collapsed onto an occupied building, trapping and injuring one woman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain developed across the area ahead of an approaching warm front, consolidating into a slow-moving band of heavy rain west of New York City by late morning. Precipitable water values increased from 1.84 on the morning sounding from Upton, NY to 2.13 by evening. Both of these values are above the 90th percentile based on a sounding climatology, with the 2.13 precipitable water value on the evening of the 25th a record for the date. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1-4 inches across New York City and Westchester County, and 2-5 inches across portions of northern Suffolk County. The ASOS at Central Park recorded 2.08 inches of rain while the ASOS at the White Plains Airport recorded 4.43 inches of rain during the event. Farther east, 4.27 inches of rain was reported by a CoCoRaHS observer in Port Jefferson Station, while a member of the public reported 4.53 inches of rain in Setauket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bronx River Parkway was closed due to flooding in White Plains between Greenacres Avenue and Tarrytown Road.

The river fell back below flood stage at 4:15am EDT on September 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped south into New York State the afternoon and evening of the 18th. This front eventually moved into northern Pennsylvania during the overnight hours from the 18th to the 19th. This front triggered a round of strong to severe thunderstorms as it moved through central New York and northeast Pennsylvania. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds which knocked down many trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees near Route 17 and Bushville Road.

The storms caused numerous trees and power lines to be knocked down, resulting in over 10,000 customers without power. A vacant historic building in Poughkeepsie, New York partially collapsed onto an occupied building, trapping and injuring one woman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New York State Mesonet site located near the town of Herkimer recorded a wind gust of 54 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed out ahead of an approaching cold front in a high precipitable water environment (around 2 inches based on the 12Z 6/28 OKX sounding), leading to isolated flash flooding across parts of Queens and Brooklyn. Close to one inch of rain fell in a short period of time over these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Greenpoint Avenue was closed due to flooding at Borden Avenue in Sunnyside.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires down on Peaceable Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eastern New York was in the midst of a heat wave on July 2nd, with a hot and humid airmass in place. A couple of isolated thunderstorms developed within the unstable environment during the evening hours, one of which became severe with wind damage reported in the town of Argyle, Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed, blocking the road on Route 40 north of Lick Springs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass brought excessively high heat indices to eastern New York from the beginning of July through the Independence Day holiday. Temperatures soared as high as 99 degrees on July 1st, the hottest day of the stretch. Combined with dewpoints in the mid-70s, heat indices reached near 110 degrees in the warmest valleys. July 5th marked the sixth consecutive day reaching a temperature of 90 degrees at Albany and Glens Falls, and a seventh consecutive day at Poughkeepsie. In addition to the hot daytime temperatures, overnight low temperatures only falling into the 70s was common, which exacerbated heat-related problems. The extensive heat prompted the opening of many cooling centers across the region.

Thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak disturbance during the evening of July 14th. A couple of these storms became severe with a few reports of downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was hot and humid on July 16th as temperatures soared into the low to mid-90s with dewpoints in the upper 60s to low 70s in the Hudson and Mohawk Valleys. This resulted in heat indices reaching the mid to upper 90s. Isolated, slow-moving thunderstorms developed during the afternoon in the hot air, one of which resulted in wind damage and flash flooding in Ulster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Atwood Road in Stone Ridge was closed due to flooding.

A hot and humid airmass brought excessively high heat indices to eastern New York from the beginning of July through the Independence Day holiday. Temperatures soared as high as 99 degrees on July 1st, the hottest day of the stretch. Combined with dewpoints in the mid-70s, heat indices reached near 110 degrees in the warmest valleys. July 5th marked the sixth consecutive day reaching a temperature of 90 degrees at Albany and Glens Falls, and a seventh consecutive day at Poughkeepsie. In addition to the hot daytime temperatures, overnight low temperatures only falling into the 70s was common, which exacerbated heat-related problems. The extensive heat prompted the opening of many cooling centers across the region.

July 5th marked the sixth consecutive day reaching a temperature of 90 degrees at Albany and Glens Falls, and a seventh consecutive day at Poughkeepsie. In addition to the hot daytime temperatures, overnight low temperatures only falling into the 70s was common, which exacerbated heat-related problems. The extensive heat prompted the opening of many cooling centers across the region.

A cold front dropped south into New York State the afternoon and evening of the 18th. This front eventually moved into northern Pennsylvania during the overnight hours from the 18th to the 19th. This front triggered a round of strong to severe thunderstorms as it moved through central New York and northeast Pennsylvania. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds which knocked down many trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees on Cohen Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite offshore winds, residual southerly swells in the wake of a cold frontal passage resulted in 1 to 3 ft surf and moderate rip current activity along the Atlantic ocean beachfront on Tuesday June 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10-year old boy drowned after being pulled out by a rip current along one of the jetties near Ocean Beach Park off Edwards Boulevardin Long Beach around 6 p.m. Tuesday evening. The boy, Ramell McRae, and his slightly older 11-year-old brother, Jaquan McRae, were in the waters at Long Beach where swimming was off-limits Tuesday because lifeguards were not on duty, according to Long Beach Fire Commissioner Scott Kemins on scene. The 11-year-old brother was taken to a hospital in Oceanside, where he's in stable condition, officials said. The Coast Guard, NYPD Aviation, Nassau County Marine and Aviation, and Wantagh, Long Beach, North Cedarhurst, Inwood and East Rockaway fire departments all searched for the 10 year old boy. The search wrapped up for the day around 9 p.m. Tuesday and resumed early Wednesday before authorities ultimately called off the search. The boy's body was recovered Sunday June 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through a hot and humid environment triggered a line of showers and thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley. In Orange County, this resulted in spotter reports of almost 2 inches of rain in 45 minutes, which resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Services reported flooding in Gardnerville due to 6-12 inches of running water over County Route 12 at Gardnerville Road. The road was briefly impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Building southerly swells ahead of an approaching frontal system resulted in 2 to 3 ft surf and moderate to high rip current activity along the Atlantic ocean beachfront on Monday June 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 31 yr old was reported missing off the waters of Long Beach after four friends took a late night swim in the dark waters. They called the Coast Guard in Jones Beach at 2 a.m. to report their 31-year-old friend was missing. Crews from multiple agencies, including searchers on a 45-foot boat, were dispatched, and a Coast Guard helicopter launched from Cape Cod, assisted by Nassau County, New York and Long Beach police and fire department in an all hands search effort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region with temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the 60s and 70s.  This resulted in heat index values reaching 100 to 110 degrees during the afternoon hours in valley areas. ||This hot and humid air mass also provided an unstable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms to form ahead of a cold front passage during the afternoon and evening hours of June 18th. The storms caused numerous trees and power lines to be knocked down, resulting in over 10,000 customers without power. A vacant historic building in Poughkeepsie, New York partially collapsed onto an occupied building, trapping and injuring one woman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed out ahead of an approaching cold front in a high precipitable water environment (around 2 inches based on the 12Z 6/28 OKX sounding), leading to isolated flash flooding across parts of Queens and Brooklyn. Close to one inch of rain fell in a short period of time over these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The junction of Kings Highway and East 53rd Street was closed due to flooding in East Flatbush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating of a very warm and humid air mass led to the development of moderate amounts of instability across the bulk of the area by late morning and early afternoon. Within this moderately unstable environment, developing lake breeze boundaries quickly forced numerous showers and thunderstorms. The storms developed first across Lewis County and Northern Cayuga Counties after 1 PM.  The best activity of the day by far developed later as a line of upstream convection over Southern Ontario pressed southeastward and interacted with the Lake Erie lake breeze boundary over the Niagara Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of trees and wires down in Lewiston, including a tree down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered severe thunderstorms across southeastern New York, they produced 3 tornadoes across Rockland, Westchester and Suffolk Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strike reported by Broadcast Media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low deepened to near 988 hPa just to the east of the southern tip of Georgian Bay early Wednesday afternoon on April 4. Damaging wind gusts occurred across the entire area with multiple trees down, wires down, and overturned semis. Multiple structure failures occurred, particularly in the North Country, where one man was killed by the collapse of a sugar shack. High winds pushed ice over the ice boom on Lake Erie and into the Niagara River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were reported down throughout the county through the event. One truck overturned in Williamsville with one injury due to the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was in an unusually warm and moist air mass with a mid-level ridge across the mid-Atlantic and an upper level trough well to the north across Hudson Bay. Surface dew points were near 70 with impressive CAPE values near 2000 J/Kg. The trigger was a subtle pre-frontal trough from upstream convection and lake breeze boundaries. Storms started to develop early in the afternoon along the lake breeze east of Lake Erie. These quickly expanded to south and east of Lake Ontario and began to accelerate eastward later in the afternoon. Numerous storms displayed impressive cores aloft on radar with damaging winds following the descent of these cores. In addition to severe weather, the 12Z Buffalo sounding had a precipitable water value of 1.75 inches, which probably reached 2 inches in some spots. Some flash flooding was the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in Cato.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down by media in Babylon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season heat wave impacted eastern New York during the first week of September. A persistent warm and moist airmass characterized by daytime highs in the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in heat index values reaching 95-105 degrees during the afternoon hours. Daily record high temperatures were set or tied at Albany on the 5th and 6th, and a daily record high minimum temperature was set on the 3rd. This heat wave coincided with the first week of school for many, and the heat prompted some schools to dismiss classes early and postpone or cancel sports practices.

A cold front well upstream of the area that extended from central Lower Michigan to southern Ontario focused the majority of the convection through the evening hours. A separate line of broken, weak thunderstorms developed over Lake Erie during the mid to late evening along a weak pre-frontal trough, possibly aided by convergence over the lake. These storms remained weak as they moved to the shoreline. One storm pulsed up very rapidly near Angola and moved inland through the Buffalo South Towns. This particular storm pulsed a couple of times through its inland life cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree reported down in the Town of Eden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was in an unusually warm and moist air mass with a mid-level ridge across the mid-Atlantic and an upper level trough well to the north across Hudson Bay. Surface dew points were near 70 with impressive CAPE values near 2000 J/Kg. The trigger was a subtle pre-frontal trough from upstream convection and lake breeze boundaries. Storms started to develop early in the afternoon along the lake breeze east of Lake Erie. These quickly expanded to south and east of Lake Ontario and began to accelerate eastward later in the afternoon. Numerous storms displayed impressive cores aloft on radar with damaging winds following the descent of these cores. In addition to severe weather, the 12Z Buffalo sounding had a precipitable water value of 1.75 inches, which probably reached 2 inches in some spots. Some flash flooding was the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in South Dayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged heat wave across our forecast area, with several of our traditionally hotter sites topping 90F for 5 to 7 straight days, heat and humidity created an unstable environment favorable for thunderstorms and flash flooding. However, the wind field was lacking, with shear around 15-20 knots between 0-6 km. Through the evening hours, a pronounced short wave aloft was absent. Storms fired through the afternoon and evening hours, on lake breeze boundaries, prior convective boundaries, etc. This resulted in slow movement of the thunderstorms in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Route 78 and Strykersville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakly forced secondary cold front crossed the North Country late in the afternoon. Storms were firing north of Lake Ontario along the boundary as it approached. As is typical, most of the storms would rapidly weaken as they crossed over the lake breeze boundary to the west of the Saint Lawrence River, except for one.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down to the southwest of Cape Vincent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed eastern New York on the 6th, bringing and end to a four-day heat wave. This front sparked a broken line of thunderstorms as it interacted with the hot and humid airmass. Some of these storms became severe, resulting in scattered reports of wind damage. Around 5500 customers lost power in Columbia and Dutchess Counties due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed along Route 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass brought excessively high heat indices to eastern New York from the beginning of July through the Independence Day holiday. Temperatures soared as high as 99 degrees on July 1st, the hottest day of the stretch. Combined with dewpoints in the mid-70s, heat indices reached near 110 degrees in the warmest valleys. July 5th marked the sixth consecutive day reaching a temperature of 90 degrees at Albany and Glens Falls, and a seventh consecutive day at Poughkeepsie. In addition to the hot daytime temperatures, overnight low temperatures only falling into the 70s was common, which exacerbated heat-related problems. The extensive heat prompted the opening of many cooling centers across the region.
A coastal storm moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elevated mesonet station measured a 60 mph gust in Bayville at 940 am. The elevation is 45 feet. Around that time, other nearby stations at lower elevations reported gusts 45 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season heat wave impacted eastern New York during the first week of September. A persistent warm and moist airmass characterized by daytime highs in the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in heat index values reaching 95-105 degrees during the afternoon hours. Daily record high temperatures were set or tied at Albany on the 5th and 6th, and a daily record high minimum temperature was set on the 3rd. This heat wave coincided with the first week of school for many, and the heat prompted some schools to dismiss classes early and postpone or cancel sports practices.

A surface low deepened to near 988 hPa just to the east of the southern tip of Georgian Bay early Wednesday afternoon on April 4. Damaging wind gusts occurred across the entire area with multiple trees down, wires down, and overturned semis. Multiple structure failures occurred, particularly in the North Country, where one man was killed by the collapse of a sugar shack. High winds pushed ice over the ice boom on Lake Erie and into the Niagara River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were reported down throughout the county through the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place across western and north central New York. As is usually the case with an approaching cold front, thunderstorms fired up along a pre-frontal boundary that extended from the Lake Erie shoreline northeast to Rochester. As the storms grew northeast along this boundary, they eventually encountered an outflow boundary pushing south from Lake Ontario. The intersecting boundaries fueled more impressive storms. Given high precipitable water values, the strong updrafts in these boundary enhanced storms generated copious amounts of lightning. Low topped convection does not allow for such electrification as a deep mixed phase layer is not present. The tight clustering of these lightning cores, when combined with strong divergence at cloud top was enough to generate storms that produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wire down on Garland Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of an approaching cold front and stalled over parts of Brooklyn and Staten Island, producing anywhere from 1-4.5 inches of rain in a matter of hours. The heaviest rain fell across Staten Island, where a mesonet in New Dorp Beach recorded 4.37 inches of rain. This resulted in numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Amboy Road and Ainsworth Avenue closed due to flooding in the Bay Terrace section of Staten Island with emergency personnel responding to a water rescue on Amboy Road.

A cold front well upstream of the area that extended from central Lower Michigan to southern Ontario focused the majority of the convection through the evening hours. A separate line of broken, weak thunderstorms developed over Lake Erie during the mid to late evening along a weak pre-frontal trough, possibly aided by convergence over the lake. These storms remained weak as they moved to the shoreline. One storm pulsed up very rapidly near Angola and moved inland through the Buffalo South Towns. This particular storm pulsed a couple of times through its inland life cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong wind field was in place ahead of a well defined cold front. This supported very high effective bulk shear values along with very high levels of helicity. All of this supported cells that quickly formed into a textbook line echo wave pattern (LEWP) which quickly moved across the area. There were a handful of spin ups within the LEWP that developed very deep mesocyclones with persistent wind threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow-moving cold front during the afternoon and evening of July 27th. The first cluster of storms that developed in the early afternoon produced fairly extensive wind damage over portions of the Capital District in addition to hail up to golf ball size. The wind brought down several large trees onto houses in and around Albany and Troy and damaged an outdoor stage set up on a golf course. Several gravestones were shattered in Albany Rural Cemetery when trees fell on them. More than 5,000 households lost power in Albany County and some were without power for up to 36 hours.||Additional strong to severe storms affected portions of the Catskills, Schoharie and Mohawk Valleys, and Mid Hudson Valley during the afternoon and evening with isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered severe thunderstorms across southeastern New York, they produced 3 tornadoes across Rockland, Westchester and Suffolk Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees reported down in a field on Bridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was in an unusually warm and moist air mass with a mid-level ridge across the mid-Atlantic and an upper level trough well to the north across Hudson Bay. Surface dew points were near 70 with impressive CAPE values near 2000 J/Kg. The trigger was a subtle pre-frontal trough from upstream convection and lake breeze boundaries. Storms started to develop early in the afternoon along the lake breeze east of Lake Erie. These quickly expanded to south and east of Lake Ontario and began to accelerate eastward later in the afternoon. Numerous storms displayed impressive cores aloft on radar with damaging winds following the descent of these cores. In addition to severe weather, the 12Z Buffalo sounding had a precipitable water value of 1.75 inches, which probably reached 2 inches in some spots. Some flash flooding was the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires down on a camp on Rainbow Showers Road near Lake Ontario in the Town of Richland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at LaGuardia Airport reported a gust to 51 mpg at 1110 am. Another CWOP station in Whitestone also measured a 51 mph wind gust at 122 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station reported a 50 mph wind gust at 955 am near Rockaway Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Saw Mill River Parkway and Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low deepened to near 988 hPa just to the east of the southern tip of Georgian Bay early Wednesday afternoon on April 4. Damaging wind gusts occurred across the entire area with multiple trees down, wires down, and overturned semis. Multiple structure failures occurred, particularly in the North Country, where one man was killed by the collapse of a sugar shack. High winds pushed ice over the ice boom on Lake Erie and into the Niagara River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were reported down throughout the county through the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season heat wave impacted eastern New York during the first week of September. A persistent warm and moist airmass characterized by daytime highs in the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in heat index values reaching 95-105 degrees during the afternoon hours. Daily record high temperatures were set or tied at Albany on the 5th and 6th, and a daily record high minimum temperature was set on the 3rd. This heat wave coincided with the first week of school for many, and the heat prompted some schools to dismiss classes early and postpone or cancel sports practices.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong wind field was in place ahead of a well defined cold front. This supported very high effective bulk shear values along with very high levels of helicity. All of this supported cells that quickly formed into a textbook line echo wave pattern (LEWP) which quickly moved across the area. There were a handful of spin ups within the LEWP that developed very deep mesocyclones with persistent wind threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

There were a handful of spin ups within the LEWP that developed very deep mesocyclones with persistent wind threats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple hardwood trees were reported down at Flatiron Road and U.S. Highway 20. Limbs were also down nearby with shingles blown off the roof of an antique shop. Damage consistent with a microburst continued east-northeast into Canandaigua that included the overturning of a mobile sign and recreational vehicle as well as trees and limbs down along North Pearl Stree and Buffalo Street Extension.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the mid-month ice storm departed, high winds and rainfall ushered warmer air into the area. This resulted in locally strong wind gusts in the North Country and wind damage, especially in Jefferson and Lewis Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of trees, branches, and wires down in multiple areas of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacting with an unseasonably warm and humid airmass sparked an area of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of the 26th. Though these storms were mainly below severe limits, one storm produced minor damage in Saratoga County. The storms did produce heavy rain, with one report of a road closure due to flash flooding in Dutchess County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 22 was closed between Rudd Pond Road and Beilke Road due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July 5th marked the sixth straight day of 90 degree temperatures at Albany, but the heat was broken by scattered showers and thunderstorms in the afternoon. One slow-moving storm dumped heavy rainfall over the central portion of Albany, resulting in numerous reports of flash flooding. Locally, up to five inches of rainfall occurred in less than two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of up to two and a half inches in less than an hour, and nearly five inches in less than two hours, brought flash flooding to the central portion of the city of Albany. The heavy rain resulted in numerous cars becoming stranded in the flood waters, several reports of basement flooding, and several inches of flood water entering a beverage store. The flooding impacted the neighborhoods of Buckingham Pond, New Scotland, and Melrose, and resulted in the closures of Western Avenue, Manning Boulevard, Central Avenue, and a portion of the Harriman State Office Campus Loop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow-moving cold front during the afternoon and evening of July 27th. The first cluster of storms that developed in the early afternoon produced fairly extensive wind damage over portions of the Capital District in addition to hail up to golf ball size. The wind brought down several large trees onto houses in and around Albany and Troy and damaged an outdoor stage set up on a golf course. Several gravestones were shattered in Albany Rural Cemetery when trees fell on them. More than 5,000 households lost power in Albany County and some were without power for up to 36 hours.||Additional strong to severe storms affected portions of the Catskills, Schoharie and Mohawk Valleys, and Mid Hudson Valley during the afternoon and evening with isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized rainfall amounts of around an inch were estimated to have fallen in about half an hour in Albany. This resulted in water nearly up to the wheel wells of cars at the Central Avenue underpass of Interstate 90. A few cars needed to be pushed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low deepened to near 988 hPa just to the east of the southern tip of Georgian Bay early Wednesday afternoon on April 4. Damaging wind gusts occurred across the entire area with multiple trees down, wires down, and overturned semis. Multiple structure failures occurred, particularly in the North Country, where one man was killed by the collapse of a sugar shack. High winds pushed ice over the ice boom on Lake Erie and into the Niagara River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were reported down throughout the county through the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on a car on Falmouth Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season heat wave impacted eastern New York during the first week of September. A persistent warm and moist airmass characterized by daytime highs in the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in heat index values reaching 95-105 degrees during the afternoon hours. Daily record high temperatures were set or tied at Albany on the 5th and 6th, and a daily record high minimum temperature was set on the 3rd. This heat wave coincided with the first week of school for many, and the heat prompted some schools to dismiss classes early and postpone or cancel sports practices.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Cyclone Florence moved into eastern New York on the 18th, resulting in a swath of two to three and a half inches of rainfall from the Schoharie Valley into the Capital District and the northern Taconics. The rainfall resulted in a couple of flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported in the basement of residences in Richmondville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary during the morning of the 12th. The rainfall was locally heavy with isolated spots in Ulster and Dutchess Counties receiving two to three inches of rain. The rain resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Main Street in Bloomington was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm air mass and warm water temperatures combined to allow convection to extend well past the diurnally normal time frame. This brought convection from Lake Erie into western New York during the nighttime hours on September 25 that moved inland through the morning on September 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a lightning strike on a house that resulted in electrical damage to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a wind gust up to 66 mph around 955 am in Amagansett. The mesonet station at Shinnecock, a gust up to 60 mph was reported at 1018 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station at Great Gull Island reported a wind gust to 65 mph at 1045 am. The site is at an elevation of 53 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a moist and unstable airmass ahead of an approaching cold front, leading to isolated flash flooding across parts of the New York City Metro area. Precipitable water values during the afternoon were around 2 inches, and the ASOS in Central Park recorded 2.22 inches of rain, the majority of which fell within a 2-3 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes blocked on the northbound ramp to the FDR Drive and East 62nd Street in Manhattan due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across the region during the afternoon, resulting in widely scattered showers and thunderstorms, primarily across the Lower Hudson Valley. With plenty of moisture available in the atmosphere, these storms resulted in isolated flash flooding across parts of northeastern Putnam County. A cooperative observer in Carmel, NY measured 1.39 inches of rain from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ludingtonville Road was closed along I-84 in Lake Carmel due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked from the Great Lakes to the Saint Lawrence Valley from the morning of the the 4th to the evening hours. This low pulled a warm front north of central New York in the morning and then a strong cold front across central New York and northeast Pennsylvania during the evening hours. This front triggered a line of severe thunderstorms that moved across central New York and northeast Pennsylvania the evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees over county Route 17 and  King Hill road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system formed on May 4th, pushing a strong cold front across eastern New York. A line of thunderstorms developed along this front in the afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage. Nearly 60,000 customers lost power as a result of the storms. Particularly intense damage occurred in the villages of Johnstown and Cambridge, the town of Hebron, and Saratoga National Historical Park, where dozens of trees were uprooted and snapped in concentrated swaths. Some of these trees fell onto cars and houses, resulting in property damage. The National Guard was deployed to assist with storm recovery in Washington County.||In the evening hours, strong non-thunderstorm winds developed behind the cold front, impacting portions of the southern Adirondacks. Winds gusted as high as 58 mph at Chestertown, and a few trees and limbs were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across Route 30A, just north of Gordon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection ahead of a cold front caused severe thunderstorm wind gusts and damage during the afternoon. This was followed by a trailing cold front with additional strong wind gusts over the Niagara Frontier and North Country through the evening hours for several hours. Measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were reported in Niagara Falls at the airport, the Oswego County Airport, Fort Drum, the Watertown Airport, and at New York State Mesonet sites in Genesee, Monroe, Wyoming, and Lewis Counties, as well.

Several trees were snapped in half as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An energetic storm system moved from the Great Lakes across the St. Lawrence Valley into Ontario/Quebec during the afternoon and evening hours of May 4th. Instability was marginal for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours with thunderstorms ahead of a cold front. However, winds were unseasonably strong in the atmosphere, accounting for some thunderstorms to produce damaging winds and there was some localized damage in non-thunderstorm winds accompanying the arrival of  colder air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree and several additional tree limbs downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system formed on May 4th, pushing a strong cold front across eastern New York. A line of thunderstorms developed along this front in the afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage. Nearly 60,000 customers lost power as a result of the storms. Particularly intense damage occurred in the villages of Johnstown and Cambridge, the town of Hebron, and Saratoga National Historical Park, where dozens of trees were uprooted and snapped in concentrated swaths. Some of these trees fell onto cars and houses, resulting in property damage. The National Guard was deployed to assist with storm recovery in Washington County.||In the evening hours, strong non-thunderstorm winds developed behind the cold front, impacting portions of the southern Adirondacks. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of large trees were downed near the intersection of County Routes 30 and 31 resulting in a road closure. Some trees fell onto barns, houses, and cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection ahead of a cold front caused severe thunderstorm wind gusts and damage during the afternoon. This was followed by a trailing cold front with additional strong wind gusts over the Niagara Frontier and North Country through the evening hours for several hours. Measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were reported in Niagara Falls at the airport, the Oswego County Airport, Fort Drum, the Watertown Airport, and at New York State Mesonet sites in Genesee, Monroe, Wyoming, and Lewis Counties, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down in Brighton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into an unusually hot and humid airmass, triggering showers and thunderstorms with near-tropical downpours. Areas of slow moving, and repeating, thunderstorm activity contributed to flash and poor drainage urban flooding across central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall over a short period of time flooded numerous streets in the City of Cortland, including Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north-south oriented stationary front extended along the spine of the Appalachian mountains through Pennsylvania and into the southern counties of New York state. A stronger cold front was pushing in from the Great Lakes. Warm and very humid air fueled this region with numerous thunderstorms for the better part of two days. Areas of significant flash flooding developed along the New York border counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding on Ellis Creek closed Route 17C.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a moist and unstable airmass ahead of an approaching cold front, leading to isolated flash flooding across parts of the New York City Metro area. Precipitable water values during the afternoon were around 2 inches, and the ASOS in Central Park recorded 2.22 inches of rain, the majority of which fell within a 2-3 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hutchinson River Parkway was closed southbound at Exit 12 in Pelham due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system formed on May 4th, pushing a strong cold front across eastern New York. A line of thunderstorms developed along this front in the afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage. Nearly 60,000 customers lost power as a result of the storms. Particularly intense damage occurred in the villages of Johnstown and Cambridge, the town of Hebron, and Saratoga National Historical Park, where dozens of trees were uprooted and snapped in concentrated swaths. Some of these trees fell onto cars and houses, resulting in property damage. The National Guard was deployed to assist with storm recovery in Washington County.||In the evening hours, strong non-thunderstorm winds developed behind the cold front, impacting portions of the southern Adirondacks. Winds gusted as high as 58 mph at Chestertown, and a few trees and limbs were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was underneath a large upper level ridge with a subtle frontal boundary near the NY/PA state line. The region was in a warm and very moist air mass with precipitable water values around 1.75 inches. Thunderstorms developed across Allegany County early in the morning. With 700 mb winds less than 10 kts, these storms hardly moved at all. They maintained intensity, eventually dropping a radar estimated 2.5 inches in about an hour. These storms eventually meandered into Northern PA, producing up to 6 inches of rain there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A male was struck by lightning along Route 244 near Amity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through with ample wind shear while in a marginal risk during the afternoon. This touched off several lines of convection. One storm of interest with notable rotation moved through Allegany State Park. A few more storms developed during the afternoon and evening hours that didn't last as long nor look as impressive on radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down at the Quaker entrance of Allegany State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A benign surface map featured a diffuse high pressure system across the mid-Atlantic states. Southwesterly flow ahead of a shortwave moving across southern Ontario advected moist and unstable air into the area. Despite ample instability, wind fields were weak, suggesting an environment favorable for pulse storms. While storms developed south of Lake Ontario in New York, the strongest storms were in Ontario.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of trees down were received from Palmyra and Macedon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms impacted portions of the eastern Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and Taconics during the afternoon of May 15th. Several supercell thunderstorms developed, with the lead storm resulting in a EF1 tornado near Saugerties and a swath of hail of two inches diameter and greater. Later in the afternoon, an intense thunderstorm with winds up to 105 mph resulted in a widespread area of wind damage along the I-84 corridor in southern Dutchess County. ||Over 35,000 customers in Dutchess and Ulster Counties lost power as a result of the storms. Travel restrictions were issued for southern Dutchess County due to downed trees and malfunctioning traffic lights, and Amtrak and Metro North rail service was suspended in the county. The governor deployed 125 members of the National Guard to aid storm recovery efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large pine trees were snapped two feet above the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure to the south pumped hot and humid air northward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 71 year old man was found dead in the driver's seat of a vehicle at 345 pm in Whitestone. Hyperthermia was the cause per an autopsy. Heat indices were in the lower to mid 90s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A benign surface map featured a diffuse high pressure system across the mid-Atlantic states. Southwesterly flow ahead of a shortwave moving across southern Ontario advected moist and unstable air into the area. Despite ample instability, wind fields were weak, suggesting an environment favorable for pulse storms. While storms developed south of Lake Ontario in New York, the strongest storms were in Ontario.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near the intersection of Route 17 and 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved across southern Ontario during the overnight and pre-dawn hours with weak forcing brushing areas close to the Canadian border. A cluster of storms developed over the western end of Lake Ontario and maintained intensity as they crossed the lake, likely due to better nocturnal instability over the very warm lake waters. Outflow from these storms came onshore near Stony Point and Sackets Harbor with greater than 40 knots velocity and a still elevated core. This core came down as the storms approached Jefferson County. The storms quickly weakened as they moved onshore and away from the warm waters of Lake Ontario.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down across Ridge Road between Route 3 and 75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was in a moist southwest flow with a weak ridge across the southeastern states and an shortwave digging into eastern Michigan. The 12z BUF sounding had a precipitable water value of 1.78 inches but very limited wind shear with 15 kts at 850mb and 20 kts at 700 mb. Despite this limited shear, an impressive storm developed across lake Erie around 7 AM and slowly tracked across the Buffalo metro area around 9 AM. The same storm moved eastward toward Victor, producing mainly heavy rainfall with radar estimates near 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were reported down with minor property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level closed low, more typical of the cold season, passed slowly through PA and into eastern NY. Abundant moisture in the presence of this anomalous forcing produced heavy rain and flash flooding. The main corridors of heavy rain developed along fairly subtle deformation zones and subtle low level convergence zones in Oswego, Wayne, and Ontario counties, reaching eastern Monroe County by mid-morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park and Woolston Roads were closed in Powder Mill Park due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain developed across the area ahead of an approaching warm front, consolidating into a slow-moving band of heavy rain west of New York City by late morning. Precipitable water values increased from 1.84 on the morning sounding from Upton, NY to 2.13 by evening. Both of these values are above the 90th percentile based on a sounding climatology, with the 2.13 precipitable water value on the evening of the 25th a record for the date. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1-4 inches across New York City and Westchester County, and 2-5 inches across portions of northern Suffolk County. The ASOS at Central Park recorded 2.08 inches of rain while the ASOS at the White Plains Airport recorded 4.43 inches of rain during the event. Farther east, 4.27 inches of rain was reported by a CoCoRaHS observer in Port Jefferson Station, while a member of the public reported 4.53 inches of rain in Setauket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding was reported across the village of Port Jefferson, with some of the most significant flooding occurring on Main Street near Maple Place. Water was 3-4 feed deep in several locations with multiple cars stuck in flood waters. The flooding resulted in people being rescued from both the Old Fields Restaurant on Wynn Lane and Theatre Three on Main Street, as well as damage to a combined retail/residential building located at 464 Main Street.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed over PA and tracked northeastward and over Allegany County. This resulted in 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall and isolated flooding. The pattern failed to evolved through the following day, resulting in a similar setup causing flooding over Chautauqua County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a house and car along Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms impacted portions of the eastern Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and Taconics during the afternoon of May 15th. Several supercell thunderstorms developed, with the lead storm resulting in a EF1 tornado near Saugerties and a swath of hail of two inches diameter and greater. Later in the afternoon, an intense thunderstorm with winds up to 105 mph resulted in a widespread area of wind damage along the I-84 corridor in southern Dutchess County. ||Over 35,000 customers in Dutchess and Ulster Counties lost power as a result of the storms. Travel restrictions were issued for southern Dutchess County due to downed trees and malfunctioning traffic lights, and Amtrak and Metro North rail service was suspended in the county. The governor deployed 125 members of the National Guard to aid storm recovery efforts.
A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across central New York during the evening hours ahead of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across central New York during the evening hours ahead of a strong cold front.

A moist environment was over the area with a deep and slow-moving closed low to the west. A cluster of thunderstorms developed over PA and tracked northeastward and over Allegany County. This resulted in 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall and isolated flooding. The pattern failed to evolved through the following day, resulting in a similar setup causing flooding over Chautauqua County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.31 reported in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex array of surface frontal boundaries and upper level disturbances produced scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Southern Tier of New York. Locally heavy rainfall contributed to isolated areas of flash flooding and ponding of water in low lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stationary thunderstorms created severe flooding of roads and properties along Hendy Creek into the village of West Elmira.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north-south oriented stationary front extended along the spine of the Appalachian mountains through Pennsylvania and into the southern counties of New York state. A stronger cold front was pushing in from the Great Lakes. Warm and very humid air fueled this region with numerous thunderstorms for the better part of two days. Areas of significant flash flooding developed along the New York border counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed Route 427 with rushing water and debris covering the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front triggered an isolated severe thunderstorm over Orange County and Staten Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down throughout town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system formed on May 4th, pushing a strong cold front across eastern New York. A line of thunderstorms developed along this front in the afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage. Nearly 60,000 customers lost power as a result of the storms. Particularly intense damage occurred in the villages of Johnstown and Cambridge, the town of Hebron, and Saratoga National Historical Park, where dozens of trees were uprooted and snapped in concentrated swaths. Some of these trees fell onto cars and houses, resulting in property damage. The National Guard was deployed to assist with storm recovery in Washington County.||In the evening hours, strong non-thunderstorm winds developed behind the cold front, impacting portions of the southern Adirondacks. Winds gusted as high as 58 mph at Chestertown, and a few trees and limbs were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9L was closed between Oneida Corners and Jenkinsville Road due to downed trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds in the wake of a cold front resulted in a seiche on Lake Champlain, causing water levels to rise over the southern basin. Localized minor to moderate flooding occurred along the lakeshore in the village of Whitehall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding occurred along the south end of Lake Champlain in the village of Whitehall. Yards were flooded along Lower Main Street and North William Street, and water partially surrounded a building at the Whitehall RV Campground. Water levels peaked during the morning of May 7th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person struck by lightning sheltering under a tree at Sunken Meadow Park resulting in one fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season heat wave impacted eastern New York during the first week of September. A persistent warm and moist airmass characterized by daytime highs in the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in heat index values reaching 95-105 degrees during the afternoon hours. Daily record high temperatures were set or tied at Albany on the 5th and 6th, and a daily record high minimum temperature was set on the 3rd. This heat wave coincided with the first week of school for many, and the heat prompted some schools to dismiss classes early and postpone or cancel sports practices.

Isolated thunderstorms developed in the Mohawk Valley during the afternoon of the 5th in a hot and humid airmass. Though these storms were mainly below severe limits, one storm produced several reports of wind damage in Broadalbin. It was reported that 380 residences lost power due to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were downed in the village of Broadalbin. One tree was downed onto a car with people trapped inside. Other trees were downed onto houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was underneath a large upper level ridge with a subtle frontal boundary near the NY/PA state line. The region was in a warm and very moist air mass with precipitable water values around 1.75 inches. Thunderstorms developed across Allegany County early in the morning. With 700 mb winds less than 10 kts, these storms hardly moved at all. They maintained intensity, eventually dropping a radar estimated 2.5 inches in about an hour. These storms eventually meandered into Northern PA, producing up to 6 inches of rain there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and associated debris over roads in Richburg, Little Genesee, and Clarksville. This included Rout 40, Route 5A, and Sunny Hill Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree was snapped near the base resulting in downed wires.
